channel_title: AI News & Strategy Daily | Nate B Jones
title: I Spent 200 Hours Teaching AI Writing—Here Are 6 Principles Everyone Gets WRONG (+ Demo Prompt)
executive_summary: In a recent exploration of AI-assisted business writing, Nate B Jones highlights that unclear thinking and standards are the real culprits behind the 'AI slop' drowning teams. He emphasizes the importance of defining concrete quality criteria and adopting an 'intent-driven' approach to writing, which can significantly improve AI-generated content. Teams that establish clear standards will thrive, while those that don't will struggle.
bullets: AI-generated documents are overwhelming teams, leading to what Nate calls 'AI slop.' This means that instead of enhancing productivity, AI is often creating confusion and low-quality outputs. It's like trying to drink from a fire hose — the sheer volume can drown you if you're not prepared.

Defining concrete quality criteria is essential for effective AI use. Without clear standards, AI will produce inconsistent results, much like a chef who doesn't follow a recipe and ends up with a dish that tastes different every time.

'Intent-driven' writing is a crucial concept to grasp. This means writing with a specific purpose in mind, which helps guide the AI in generating relevant content. Think of it like a GPS that needs a clear destination to give you the right directions — without that, you could end up lost.

Nate suggests using AI not just for writing but also for evaluation. This dual approach can streamline processes and improve quality control. It's like having a coach who not only trains you but also reviews your performance to help you improve.

Teams that articulate clear standards and goals will thrive with AI, while those that don't will drown in AI slop. This is akin to a ship sailing with a clear map versus one that is adrift in the ocean without direction.

Nate emphasizes that 'a good human engineer wrote this' is becoming a weaker security claim. This highlights the need for accountability and clarity in AI-generated content, similar to how a chef must take responsibility for the meal they serve.

Nate's insights reveal that most teams currently have their evaluation tests split backwards, with 80% focusing on functionality and only 20% on code hygiene. This imbalance can lead to serious issues, like a car that runs well but has dangerous brakes — it might work, but it’s not safe.

He points out that implementation costs for AI tools are going to zero, making them more accessible than ever. This is like the price of smartphones dropping, allowing more people to own one and benefit from the technology.

Nate stresses the importance of setting a rule that at least 50% of evaluation tests must check for code hygiene. This ensures that teams are not just checking if the code works but also if it's well-structured and maintainable. Ignoring this could lead to a messy codebase that’s hard to fix later.

He mentions that teams are currently drowning in AI-generated documents, which can lead to wasted time and resources. It's like trying to find a needle in a haystack when there’s too much clutter — you need to clear the mess to find what’s valuable.

Nate's video suggests that teams should focus on defining their quality criteria within the next 4-5 months to see significant improvements. This timeline is crucial; delaying this could mean falling further behind in the competitive landscape of AI writing.
The concept of 'AI slop' refers to the poor quality of AI outputs when clear standards are lacking. It's like a painter who splashes paint without a plan — the result is chaotic and unappealing.
key_takeaways: Define concrete quality criteria for AI-generated content within the next 4-5 months. This clarity will help your team produce better outputs and avoid the pitfalls of 'AI slop.' If you skip this step, you risk drowning in low-quality documents.

Set a rule that at least 50% of your evaluation tests must check for code hygiene. This will ensure that your code is not only functional but also clean and maintainable. Ignoring this could lead to a messy codebase that becomes difficult to manage over time.

Adopt an 'intent-driven' writing approach for all AI-generated content. This will guide the AI in producing relevant and purposeful outputs. Without this focus, you may end up with content that misses the mark and wastes time.

technical_terms.AI slop: This refers to the poor quality and confusion resulting from unclear standards in AI-generated content. Think of it like a messy kitchen where ingredients are everywhere — you can't cook a good meal in that chaos.
technical_terms.Intent-driven writing: This is writing with a specific purpose in mind, guiding AI to produce relevant content. Like a GPS needs a destination to give proper directions, intent-driven writing helps ensure the AI stays on track.
technical_terms.Quality criteria: These are specific standards that define what makes AI-generated content acceptable. Think of it like a checklist for a pilot before takeoff — without it, you risk a dangerous flight.
technical_terms.Code hygiene: This term refers to the cleanliness and organization of code, ensuring it is easy to read and maintain. Like keeping your room tidy, good code hygiene makes it easier to find things and make improvements.
technical_terms.Evaluation tests: These are assessments used to check the quality and functionality of code or AI outputs. Think of them like a health check-up — they help identify issues before they become serious problems.
technical_terms.Implementation costs: This refers to the expenses associated with deploying AI tools and systems. Like the cost of setting up a new business, knowing these costs helps in budgeting and planning.
technical_terms.AI-generated documents: These are texts produced by AI systems, often requiring human oversight for quality. Think of them like automated emails — they can save time but need to be checked for clarity and tone.
technical_terms.Accountability: This is the responsibility taken for the quality and outcomes of AI-generated content. Like a chef who must ensure their dish is safe to eat, accountability ensures AI outputs meet standards.
technical_terms.Performance review: This is an evaluation of how well a team or individual is doing in their tasks. Like a teacher grading a student's work, performance reviews help identify strengths and areas for improvement.
technical_terms.Quality control: This is the process of ensuring that products meet certain standards before they are released. Think of it like a final inspection on a car before it leaves the factory — it ensures everything is working properly.
technical_terms.Clarity: This refers to the quality of being easy to understand. Like a clear window that lets in light, clarity in communication helps prevent misunderstandings.
technical_terms.Standards: These are established requirements or criteria that must be met. Think of them like the rules of a game — they help everyone know how to play fairly.
